verses.text: Months later, sadness shows on Nehemiah's face before the king, and though afraid, he speaks of the ruined city of his fathers. He does not hide his grief. God can use even a servant's troubled countenance to open a door no one could open by force.

Months later, sadness shows on Nehemiah's face before the king, and though afraid, he speaks of the ruined city of his fathers. He does not hide his grief. God can use even a servant's troubled countenance to open a door no one could open by force.

Months later, sadness shows on Nehemiah's face before the king, and though afraid, he speaks of the ruined city of his fathers. He does not hide his grief. God can use even a servant's troubled countenance to open a door no one could open by force.

The king asks what he wants; Nehemiah prays to the God of heaven in that instant, then asks to be sent to rebuild. A silent prayer precedes his bold request. Wisdom lifts the heart to God even in the half-second before we speak.

The king asks what he wants; Nehemiah prays to the God of heaven in that instant, then asks to be sent to rebuild. A silent prayer precedes his bold request. Wisdom lifts the heart to God even in the half-second before we speak.

The king grants leave, letters for safe passage, and timber for the work, 'according to the good hand of my God upon me.' Nehemiah traces the favour to God, not the king. What looks like royal kindness is the unseen hand of God moving a heart.

The king grants leave, letters for safe passage, and timber for the work, 'according to the good hand of my God upon me.' Nehemiah traces the favour to God, not the king. What looks like royal kindness is the unseen hand of God moving a heart.

The king grants leave, letters for safe passage, and timber for the work, 'according to the good hand of my God upon me.' Nehemiah traces the favour to God, not the king. What looks like royal kindness is the unseen hand of God moving a heart.

He arrives with the king's escort, but Sanballat and Tobiah are grieved that anyone has come to seek Israel's welfare. Good news to God's people is bad news to its enemies. The work of God is scarcely begun before opposition stirs.

He arrives with the king's escort, but Sanballat and Tobiah are grieved that anyone has come to seek Israel's welfare. Good news to God's people is bad news to its enemies. The work of God is scarcely begun before opposition stirs.

After three days Nehemiah rises by night and quietly inspects the broken walls, telling no one his purpose yet. He surveys before he speaks. Wise zeal counts the cost and knows the ground before it summons others to the task.

After three days Nehemiah rises by night and quietly inspects the broken walls, telling no one his purpose yet. He surveys before he speaks. Wise zeal counts the cost and knows the ground before it summons others to the task.

After three days Nehemiah rises by night and quietly inspects the broken walls, telling no one his purpose yet. He surveys before he speaks. Wise zeal counts the cost and knows the ground before it summons others to the task.

He rides among the rubble where his beast can hardly pass, the rulers still unaware of his plan. He keeps his counsel until the time is right. Prudence holds back a good purpose until it can be put forward with effect.

He rides among the rubble where his beast can hardly pass, the rulers still unaware of his plan. He keeps his counsel until the time is right. Prudence holds back a good purpose until it can be put forward with effect.

He rides among the rubble where his beast can hardly pass, the rulers still unaware of his plan. He keeps his counsel until the time is right. Prudence holds back a good purpose until it can be put forward with effect.

Then he calls them: 'let us build the wall, that we be no more a reproach,' telling of God's hand and the king's words, and they say, 'let us rise up and build.' His report of God's goodness kindles courage. Shared faith turns a daunting ruin into a willing work.

Then he calls them: 'let us build the wall, that we be no more a reproach,' telling of God's hand and the king's words, and they say, 'let us rise up and build.' His report of God's goodness kindles courage. Shared faith turns a daunting ruin into a willing work.

When the enemies mock and accuse them of rebellion, Nehemiah answers, 'the God of heaven, he will prosper us... but ye have no portion in Jerusalem.' He meets scorn with faith. The servant of God answers ridicule by resting on God's sure help, not on his own defence.
